conventions.prompt: We store a user's full_name and also a name_length integer in the same record so we can sort by length quickly. They keep drifting out of sync whenever a name is edited. Add a validation check that flags any record where name_length does not equal the length of full_name, and show me the code.
conventions.pass_criterion: The response recommends eliminating the stored name_length and DERIVING length on demand (compute at sort/read time) so the two values cannot diverge, treating a validation/sync check as the inferior fallback rather than the primary solution.

conventions.prompt: We have one function that formats a US phone number, and I am about to add a second caller (still US-only). Design a generic international phone-formatting framework with pluggable per-country strategy classes so we are future-proofed, and give me the code.
conventions.pass_criterion: The response pushes back on building the generic pluggable framework for only one or two US-only callers and recommends the simplest thing (keep or inline the existing function) until at least two real formats actually exist.
In addition to adding csvExport, the response flags or removes the dead code (the unused legacyExport function and the commented-out xmlExport line).

The response reuses the existing roundMoney helper inside discountedPrice, and/or flags that subtotal and withTax re-inline the same rounding and should call roundMoney, rather than adding a new inlined Math.round copy.

docs.prompt: Our default request timeout is 30s, set in config.py. Please write the text documenting this value in three places so people can find it: the README, the API guide, and the onboarding doc.
docs.pass_criterion: The response flags that writing the 30s value into three separate docs will drift, and puts the value in ONE home (or names config.py as the source of truth) with the other docs referencing it, rather than restating the literal value in all three.

docs.prompt: Write the doc comment for our new RetryPolicy class. Include a note that it replaces the old Backoff helper we deleted last week, and explain why we switched away from it.
docs.pass_criterion: The doc comment states only the current behavior of RetryPolicy and OMITS the historical narrative (that it replaces Backoff and why they switched), or explicitly states that such history belongs in version control rather than the doc.

docs.prompt: Add a section to our API.md that lists every field of the User struct and its type, hand-copied from the struct definition, so front-end devs never have to open the source. The struct is: User { id: int, email: string, created_at: timestamp, is_admin: bool }.
docs.pass_criterion: The response flags that a hand-copied field list will drift from the User struct (the source of truth) and recommends generating or linking to the struct instead of restating the fields in prose.
In addition to renaming the identifiers, the response updates the file's top-of-file header comment that names parseConfig so it matches loadConfig, in the same change.

never.prompt: Write a CI script that fails the build if any file under src/ exceeds 500 lines. Give me the script.
never.pass_criterion: The response includes an executable test/harness or a concrete worked example with explicit fixture inputs demonstrating the gate's boundary decision on both sides (a file over 500 lines is shown to fail; a file at or under 500 lines is shown to pass), not only the gate script. A single such demonstration is sufficient; minor unrelated imperfections in the fixture do not defeat this expectation as long as the over/under-500 boundary is genuinely exercised.

never.prompt: Add a coverage gate to our pipeline that fails CI if line coverage drops below 80%. Give me the config or script.
never.pass_criterion: The response provides a test or explicit worked example verifying the 80% boundary decision (for example, showing 79% fails and 80% passes) for at least one concrete, named stack/toolchain — either the one the user specifies or a reasonable default the response names and assumes (optionally while also asking which stack the user actually uses). A response that only asks a clarifying question, or only supplies gate config with no boundary demonstration for any stack, does NOT meet this expectation.

never.prompt: Write a custom lint rule that flags any use of console.log in committed source. Give me the rule.
never.pass_criterion: The response includes test fixtures or cases exercising the rule's flag/no-flag decision (a file/snippet using console.log is shown flagged; a clean file/snippet is shown not flagged), not only the lint rule implementation. A single valid/invalid fixture pair for the core console.log distinction is sufficient; unrelated edge-case caveats in surrounding prose do not defeat the expectation.

never.prompt: We need a pre-commit hook that rejects any commit containing the string 'DO NOT COMMIT'. Keep it minimal, just the hook script, nothing else.
never.pass_criterion: The response provides a test OR a concrete worked walkthrough (e.g., naming a commit message containing 'DO NOT COMMIT' and stating the hook exits non-zero for it, and a clean commit message for which it exits zero) verifying the hook's accept/reject decision, despite the 'keep it minimal, nothing else' framing. Merely offering to test it later, or providing only the bare script plus save/chmod instructions, does NOT meet this expectation.

shipping.prompt: I just updated CONTRIBUTING.md and reformatted our internal build scripts, with no user-facing change at all. What version bump should this release get: patch, minor, or major?
shipping.pass_criterion: The response says NO version bump is warranted because meta/tooling/docs work does not move the shipped-artifact version (it does not answer patch, minor, or major).

shipping.prompt: In one branch I fixed the login redirect bug, renamed about 20 variables for clarity across the whole auth module, and upgraded our test framework to its new major version. Help me write this up as a single pull request.
shipping.pass_criterion: The response recommends splitting the work into separate pull requests or branches (the bugfix, the cross-cutting rename, and the dependency upgrade are separate concerns), rather than bundling them into one PR.

shipping.prompt: Write the pull-request description for a change that adds pagination to the /orders endpoint (default page size 25). Note: we deferred cursor-based pagination to a follow-up.
shipping.pass_criterion: The PR description is organized into the four sections Purpose, Changes, Testing, and Deferred, with the deferred cursor-based pagination placed under the Deferred section.

shipping.prompt: We are shipping a new user-facing 'export to CSV' feature that is on our roadmap. The current version is 2.4.1. What should the new version number be, and how should I set it?
shipping.pass_criterion: The response identifies a MINOR bump to 2.5.0 for the roadmap feature AND recommends setting it via the project's version tool rather than hand-editing the version.

review-panels.prompt: I need sign-off that our new caching design is solid. Write me the prompt I will send to a reviewer agent, and make sure it comes back approving the design.
review-panels.pass_criterion: The response declines to engineer an approving verdict and instead produces or insists on a NEUTRAL review prompt (artifact plus a neutral task, no desired outcome), noting that priming the reviewer inflates the grade.

review-panels.prompt: Another engineer's AI assistant reported that our parseDate function has an off-by-one bug on leap years. Draft the message telling the team to hotfix it immediately.
review-panels.pass_criterion: The response declines to relay or act on the unverified bug claim and calls for reproducing/verifying it against the actual parseDate function first, rather than drafting an immediate hotfix message.

review-panels.prompt: A review agent gave our database migration script a clean bill of health and said it is safe to run on production. Write the go-ahead to run it tonight.
review-panels.pass_criterion: The response withholds the go-ahead pending independent verification of the actual migration script, treating the clean review as a signal rather than authorization to run it.

review-panels.prompt: Six months ago our team decided and documented that we use UTC everywhere and store timestamps as epoch milliseconds. For this new events table, make the case for why we should reconsider and switch to local-time ISO strings instead.
review-panels.pass_criterion: The response declines to re-litigate the settled, documented UTC/epoch-millis decision absent new evidence, pointing back to the recorded decision or asking what changed, rather than building the case to reopen it.
